reasoning: The permission_callback for POST /wp-json/omnisend/v1/connect uses is_user_logged_in() (confirmed in omnisend_register_connection_routes(), lines 102-114 of class-omnisend-core-bootstrap.php), meaning any authenticated user can reach the handler. However, the handler omnisend_post_connection() (class-connection.php lines 215-311) immediately gates on current_user_can('manage_options') and returns a structured error response before any state-changing code executes. A second independent guard (wp_verify_nonce on $_POST['action_nonce'] against 'connect') would also block any non-admin who somehow bypassed the capability check, since the nonce is only rendered to admin pages. No privileged state change (set_api_key, set_brand_id, set_store_connected, wp_schedule_event) is reachable by a subscriber. The finding correctly identifies a defense-in-depth / code-quality issue (permission check in wrong layer per WordPress REST API best practices) but there is no actual exploit path: a subscriber receives an application-level 200 error response and nothing more. OWASP Broken Access Control requires that a lower-privilege principal can perform an action beyond their intended permissions — that condition is not met here. The 'medium' severity is not warranted; the only impact is route enumeration (trivially discoverable via /wp-json/ anyway) and a 200-with-error response body instead of a 401/403.
